title: Pulled butter toffee
subtitle: Hard-crack toffee worked by hand until pale and silky
excerpt: Old-fashioned English pulled toffee: a butter and golden syrup mixture cooked to hard crack, then repeatedly stretched and folded while still warm until it turns pale and develops a light, snapping texture. Cut into pieces once hard. About 500 g.
sourceNotes: Pulled toffee is documented in British confectionery writing from at least the early nineteenth century. Pre-1928 recipes appear in household confectionery guides of the period; the pulling technique for toffee is attested in pre-1928 British confectionery manuals and domestic science textbooks available through the British Library. Cross-referenced with pre-1928 British confectionery writing in the British Newspaper Archive.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Keeps wrapped individually in greaseproof paper in an airtight tin for 2 weeks. Store in a cool, dry place.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: Stovetop only. Target: 150 to 155°C (hard crack stage). A sugar thermometer is essential. The sugar is handled hot during pulling; protect hands with oiled heatproof gloves or work swiftly with well-buttered hands.

glossaryTerms.definition: A technique in which cooked hard-crack sugar is repeatedly stretched and folded while still pliable to incorporate air and change the texture. As it is pulled, the mass lightens in colour from amber to cream and develops a silky sheen; the final set is lighter and more brittle than unpulled toffee of the same recipe. Pulling must begin while the sugar is still warm enough to stretch without cracking, and must finish before it cools enough to become rigid, giving a working window of roughly 10 to 15 minutes. Oil or butter the surface and your hands before starting to prevent sticking.

content.text: Pulled toffee is distinguished from ordinary hard toffee by the

applied while the sugar is still warm: the mass lightens, aerates, and develops a snapping rather than shattering bite. The sugar work requires full attention; the mass is very hot throughout handling. About 500 g.

items.prepNote: plus extra for greasing hands and tray

content.text: Grease a large baking tray generously with butter. Combine the sugar, golden syrup, butter, water, and cream of tartar if using in a heavy-based saucepan. Stir over low heat until the sugar dissolves, then increase to medium-high and cook without stirring until the thermometer reads 152°C. Pour immediately onto the greased tray in a thin layer. Do not scrape the pan.

Leave the poured toffee for 10 to 15 minutes until the edges have cooled and the centre is still warm and pliable but not liquid. Butter your hands well. Fold the edges inward and gather the mass into a rough ball. Begin pulling: stretch it out to about 60 cm, fold it back on itself, turn, and stretch again. Repeat for 10 to 12 minutes until the toffee is pale cream in colour and has a silky, satin sheen. Work quickly; once it cools below a workable temperature it will snap rather than stretch.

While still pliable, roll the pulled toffee into a rope about 1.5 cm thick on the buttered tray. Use oiled scissors or a sharp knife to snip or cut into 3 cm pieces. Leave on greaseproof paper to cool and harden completely, about 1 hour. Wrap each piece individually in greaseproof paper once hard.
